title: Harness Lab
subtitle: Workshop operating system for working with AI agents
principles: Work so another team can continue

phases.label: Opening and orientation
phases.goal: Open the day as a shared start: why harness engineering matters right now, what the room will do from the first beat to the reveal, and how we will know the work can stand without improvised rescue.
phases.roomSummary: The room should feel immediately that this is not a prompting hackathon. It is a day of steering agents, building with them in a real repo, and later testing what survives without us.
facilitatorPrompts: We are not starting with a tool demo or a prompting contest. We are starting a day of steering work with coding agents so something durable stays behind.
You will learn, build, hand off, and inherit. That day arc is the point, not workshop logistics.
We are not learning to prompt better. We are learning to build a repo and workflow where the agent and the next team can continue safely.
Whatever stays only in table talk will not survive the afternoon.
watchFors: The opening slips into rules before it offers ambition and a reason to care.
The room reads the day as a prompting contest or a feature race.
The facilitator explains the continuation mechanic too early instead of simply setting the bar for continuation.
checkpointQuestions: Where would the next team find this without you?
What is actually verified here?
What should the room carry into Build Phase 1?

facilitatorRunner.goal: Open the day as a shared room start, not as an operating brief.
say: We are not starting with a tool demo or a prompting contest.
You will learn, build, hand off, and inherit. That arc is the point of the day.
We are not learning to prompt better. We are learning to build a repo and workflow where the agent and the next team can continue safely.
show: Run the launch arc from framing through analogy and room activation into the first working contract.
Show the participant surface only briefly at the end of opening or in the talk bridge.
do: Do a fast room grouping by current experience with AI agents and let two short voices land.
Keep the launch brisk: each beat should change the room state, not explain the whole workshop.
watch: The opening must not turn into an internal operating memo.
The room should feel the ambition of the day before it gets the first rule.
fallback: If time slips, keep the framing, the analogy, and the first contract. Shorten the activation to a quick show of hands.
If energy drops, return to the line that the afternoon will test what remains without rescue.

scenes.label: Framing
scenes.title: Today you learn to shape the work so anyone — or any agent — can carry it
scenes.body: That's the craft underneath all the agent hype. Not better prompts. Not faster typing. A repo, a workflow, a context that carries the next move without you standing over it. By tonight you'll have built one with your hands, handed it to another team, and seen what survives. The next day you open a coding agent, you'll know what to do differently.
facilitatorNotes: Open with ambition and the participant destination, not logistics.
The hero title is the day's spine — read it out loud, then pause.
Cut any reference to prompt theatre or contest framing.

blocks.title: Today you learn to shape the work so anyone — or any agent — can carry it
blocks.body: That's the craft underneath all the agent hype. Not better prompts. Not faster typing. A repo, a workflow, a context that carries the next move without you standing over it. By tonight you'll have built one with your hands, handed it to another team, and seen what survives. The next day you open a coding agent, you'll know what to do differently.

blocks.title: The main line for today
blocks.body: We are not learning to prompt better. We are learning to build a working system that carries the next move — whether the next reader is a teammate who joined today or a fresh agent you open the next day.

blocks.title: What should change today
blocks.body: The next morning you open a fresh agent in your own repo. That agent arrives without your memory. What you practice today is how you treat every session — not once, for every session.

scenes.label: Day arc
scenes.title: Learn. Build. Hand off. Continue.
scenes.body: One day, four moves. Each one sets up the next. By the time the day closes, you will have practiced each move end to end.
facilitatorNotes: Give each step one sentence. Keep the spine tight.
No rules yet — just the promise of the arc.

blocks.title: Learn. Build. Hand off. Continue.
blocks.body: This is not a prompting contest. It is a day where you will learn the craft, build something real, hand it to another team, and watch what continues without you.

items.title: Learn.
items.body: The talk and the demo name the craft and show you what it looks like in a real repo.

items.title: Build.
items.body: Your team picks a brief and builds a working slice — with AGENTS.md as a map, a plan, a tracer, and a next-move note.

items.title: Hand off.
items.body: After lunch, your repo is not yours anymore. Another team inherits it cold. You inherit theirs.

items.title: Continue.
items.body: You continue in the inherited repo — reading, capturing friction, shipping another slice. The afternoon tests what the morning encoded.

scenes.label: Team formation (room)
scenes.title: Form the line. Count off. Claim your anchor.
scenes.body: Wall scene that holds for the full nine minutes of team formation.
facilitatorNotes: Hold this on the wall for the full nine minutes of team formation.
The wall holds the shared steps for the room; facilitate the sequence live from the front.
Advance to the handoff only once every team has both a name and an anchor on the board.

blocks.title: Form the line. Count off. Claim your anchor.
blocks.body: Everyone stands. Experience line along the wall. Count off into mixed teams. Sit, introduce, name the team, anchor it.

items: 3 · Sit, introduce, name the team (4 min)
5 · Raise a hand — we record the team live

blocks.title: Follow the room.
blocks.body: This screen is the shared anchor while the facilitator forms the teams live.

scenes.label: Handoff to the talk
scenes.title: Your team is on the board. Look up.
scenes.body: Brief transition beat. Facilitator moves the room into the talk.
facilitatorNotes: 30-second beat. Room reset, then straight into the talk.
May be collapsed into a verbal transition if the team formation runs long.

blocks.title: Your team is on the board. Look up.
blocks.body: Next: the talk. We start by naming the craft underneath all the agent hype.

participantMoments.label: Look up and orient
participantMoments.title: Stay with the room
participantMoments.body: No action yet. Listen for the shape of the day and the bar for what must survive without rescue.

blocks.title: Stay with the room
blocks.body: No action yet. Listen for the day arc and the standard for work that survives handoff.

participantMoments.label: Look up
participantMoments.title: Your team is recorded. Look up.
participantMoments.body: The room is moving into the talk. Pause the table setup and switch your attention back to the front.

blocks.title: Your team is recorded. Look up.
blocks.body: Team is set. The room is moving into the talk.

phases.label: The Craft Underneath
phases.goal: Turn the opening energy into a precise thesis: harness engineering is team infrastructure for working with agents, and the first build move must begin with map, boundaries, and proof rather than another prompt.
phases.roomSummary: By the end of the talk, the room should see AGENTS.md, skills, runbooks, and explicit verification as working infrastructure rather than optional polish, and Build Phase 1 should feel like the obvious next move.
facilitatorPrompts: We are not learning to prompt better. We are learning to build a repo and workflow where an agent and another team can continue safely.
If it is not in the repo, it does not exist.
When the agent does more, you need to verify better.
After this talk, teams should return to the repo with a concrete operating system, not with a search for a smarter prompt.
watchFors: The talk turns into a tool feature tour.
Teams hear prompting tips instead of workflow discipline.
There is no clean bridge into the first build phase.
checkpointQuestions: What will you move from your head or from chat into the repo today?
Which repo signal would help another team continue?
Which explicit check will be your trust boundary today?

facilitatorRunner.goal: Make harness engineering precise, memorable, and immediately actionable so the room returns to the repo with a concrete first working contract.
say: Open with the repo-readiness contrast: same prompt, two repos. Let the room see the difference before you name it.
After the contrast, ask the room: 'What changed?' Let two voices answer.
Name what you saw: the agent in the bare repo made plausible but wrong decisions because it had no constraints. This is task drift.
Context is leverage, not cosmetics. The repo did the work, not the prompt.
show: Show the repo-readiness contrast first, then the thesis scene, then the bridge into build.
Use the participant surface only as proof that the room, dashboard, and repo still describe the same moment.
do: Run the contrast: show the bare repo variant first, then the harnessed variant with the same prompt.
After the contrast lands, transition to the thesis scene. Say the thesis once clearly.
Send the room into Build Phase 1 with one expectation: map and verification first, feature motion second.
watch: The talk must not collapse into prompt tips or tool theatrics.
By the end, the room should know what to do in the repo in the next few minutes.
fallback: If live demo drags, use pre-prepared screenshots. The contrast matters more than live generation.
If attention drops, keep the core line, the team-lead analogy, and one concrete build expectation.

scenes.label: Something changed
scenes.title: While we argued about prompts, something changed
scenes.body: Six months ago the conversation was still about prompts. Today the serious voices are talking about the system around the agent — what it reads, what bounds it, what proves the work.
facilitatorNotes: Evidence scene only. Deliberate pause after each named voice.
If time is tight, cut to Lopopolo, Böckeler, and Stripe.

blocks.title: While we argued about prompts, something changed
blocks.body: The same pattern showed up fast: Lopopolo shipping at scale, Böckeler naming harness engineering, Guo naming the role shift, Stripe proving it in production.

scenes.label: Why this matters now
scenes.title: Why this matters now
scenes.body: No one can tell you what agentic coding looks like by the end of 2026. That uncertainty is exactly why the craft matters now.
facilitatorNotes: Keep this scene short. It is urgency, not another evidence parade.

blocks.title: Why this matters now
blocks.body: Nobody actually knows what the end of 2026 looks like. The models will move. The repo contract around the agent is what survives the movement.

blocks.title: The hedge is not prediction.
blocks.body: You do not need to know which model wins next. You need context, boundaries, and checks that survive the next model.

scenes.label: The four pillars
scenes.title: How you actually build one
scenes.body: Fowler splits it into guides and sensors. OpenAI talks about context, constraints, and feedback. Here is the four-pillar version you can actually build with before lunch.
facilitatorNotes: Pillars scene only. The role-shift teaching now lives in the next scene.
If time is tight, collapse guides and sensors into one sentence.

blocks.title: How you actually build one
blocks.body: Four pillars. Four moves. All visible in the repo.

items.title: Context as infrastructure.
items.body: AGENTS.md, skills, runbooks. If it isn't in a file, the agent cannot rely on it.

items.title: Guides — steering before it acts.
items.body: Rules, templates, and constraints that steer before the agent acts.

items.title: Sensors — catching after it acts.
items.body: Tracer bullets, end-to-end checks, and review that catch drift after the agent acts.

items.title: Managing, not chatting.
items.body: Set direction, scope, and verification. Do not type every move live.

scenes.label: Managing, not chatting
scenes.title: Managing agents changes your role
scenes.body: The tool is not the shift. Your role is.
facilitatorNotes: Role-shift scene. Keep it crisp and human, not managerial jargon.

blocks.title: Managing agents changes your role
blocks.body: You stop feeding the agent line by line and become the person who builds the room it can work inside.

blocks.quote: I'm moving away from chatting with AIs and moving towards managing them.

blocks.title: A team lead builds the system the team runs in.
blocks.body: Rules, rituals, feedback loops, next moves. That is the job today too.

scenes.label: You've been sitting inside one
scenes.title: You've been sitting inside one all morning
scenes.body: The workshop skill. The dashboard. The participant board in front of you. The repo you're about to open. I built all of it with agents, using the discipline we just named. Not as a sales pitch — as proof. The craft is real. It works. Today we're practicing inside an example of it.
facilitatorNotes: Facilitator transition line: "Let me show you what I mean — here'  what a working agent session actually looks like when the harness is doing its job."

blocks.title: You've been sitting inside one all morning
blocks.body: The workshop skill, the dashboard, the participant board, the repo you're about to open — all of it was built with agents, using the discipline we just named. What you're about to see in the demo is a working instance, not a promise.

participantMoments.label: Stay with the room
participantMoments.title: Listen for the shift
participantMoments.body: No table action yet. Track the contrast: prompts are not the system; the repo contract around the agent is.

blocks.title: Listen for the shift
blocks.body: No table action yet. Stay with the argument and the contrast.

participantMoments.label: Name one gap
participantMoments.title: Find the weakest signal in your repo
participantMoments.body: Pick the one gap your team already suspects. Don't solve it yet. Name it cleanly so Build 1 starts from something real.

blocks.title: Find the weakest signal in your repo
blocks.body: Pick the one gap your team already suspects. The poll is a quick room signal, not a quiz.

poll.prompt: Which part of your repo is weakest right now?

participantMoments.label: Get ready for Build 1
participantMoments.title: Return to the repo with one map, one boundary, one proof target
participantMoments.body: You are about to go back to the repo. Carry one explicit next move: what will map the work, what will bound it, and what will prove it.

blocks.title: Return to the repo with one map, one boundary, one proof target
blocks.body: Build 1 should start from one explicit operating contract, not from more prompt exploration.

phases.label: Let me show you
phases.goal: Use one compact story to show a real working line: context, bounded next steps, implementation, change control, and explicit fallback moves when something goes wrong.
phases.roomSummary: The room should see quality rise as context, bounded next steps, and change control enter the flow. The point is not magic output, model mystique, or a feature parade.
facilitatorPrompts: One story-driven example, not a feature list.
The tool alone is not enough. The working system around it is what matters.
Review is not an emergency brake at the end. It is part of the workflow.
watchFors: The demo runs long and waits on generation.
It shows five modes of work instead of one clear line.
There are no explicit closure points after AGENTS.md, /plan, and /review.
checkpointQuestions: What in this flow carries intent and constraints?
Where can you see that change control is part of the process?
What from this do you need to be able to build in your own repo?

facilitatorRunner.goal: Use one compact story to show a real working line: context, bounded next steps, implementation, change control, and explicit fallback moves when something goes wrong.
say: One story-driven example, not a feature list.
The tool alone is not enough. The working system around it is what matters.
Review is not an emergency brake at the end. It is part of the workflow.
watch: The demo runs long and waits on generation.
It shows five modes of work instead of one clear line.
There are no explicit closure points after AGENTS.md, /plan, and /review.

scenes.label: Same prompt, two repos
scenes.title: Same prompt. Two repos. Watch what happens.
scenes.body: I'm going to run the same simple prompt in two repos — one bare, one with a harness. Same words. Same model. Watch what changes.
facilitatorNotes: Live demo. Pre-prepared screenshots of both Folder A drift and Folder B aligned output must be ready on a second screen.
If live generation exceeds ~90 seconds, switch to screenshots mid-demo.

sourceRefs.label: Demo script: Let me show you

blocks.title: Same prompt. Two repos. Watch what happens.
blocks.body: I'm going to run the same simple prompt in two repos — one bare, one with a harness. Same words. Same model. Watch what changes.

items.title: Folder A — the bare repo.
items.body: Project brief, nothing else. No AGENTS.md. No constraints. No plan. Most repos look exactly like this when you open them.

items.title: Folder B — the same repo, with a harness.
items.body: Same brief. Plus AGENTS.md (Goal, Context, Constraints, Done When). Plus a short plan. Plus a workshop support path ready if the team wants it.

blocks.title: That was task drift.
blocks.body: The agent started without constraints and made plausible but wrong decisions. Without a harness, it filled in the blanks with its own assumptions. This is what happens in every repo without AGENTS.md. It has a name: task drift. Once you can name it, you can build for it.

blocks.title: The prompt didn't change. The repo did.
blocks.body: Same words, same model, different harness, different outcome. That's the whole day in thirty seconds.

scenes.label: The full flow
scenes.title: Now watch it hold together
scenes.body: I'm staying in Folder B. I'm going to run the actual workflow — the one you'll be running in twenty minutes. Four beats. Watch how each one feeds the next.
facilitatorNotes: Keep the beats crisp. One pass, four moves.

sourceRefs.label: Demo script: Let me show you

blocks.title: Now watch it hold together
blocks.body: Four beats. Each one feeds the next. This is the workflow you'll run in your own Folder B in twenty minutes.

items.title: AGENTS.md — the map.
items.body: Goal, Context, Constraints, Done When. Four headings. The agent reads this first, every session. This is the team's memory.

items.title: /plan — the next safe step.
items.body: The agent breaks the work into bounded steps. You see the plan before anything is written.

items.title: Small slice.
items.body: The agent implements one bounded piece. Not the whole feature. Small enough to verify.

items.title: /review — change control inside the flow.
items.body: Review is not a panic brake at the end. It's how the agent and the team agree on what just shipped.

blocks.title: Review is not an emergency brake.
blocks.body: It's part of the workflow. Every beat feeds the next. When something goes wrong — and things go wrong — you catch it one beat away, not ten.

scenes.label: Your toolkit
scenes.title: Your toolkit, same discipline
scenes.body: You've been watching me work in Folder B. In twenty minutes, you'll be working in your own Folder B. Here are the support surfaces for today. The participant board keeps everyone moving; the workshop skill is the fast lane if your setup is ready. Let me show you that path on my machine so you see exactly what it gives you.
facilitatorNotes: Live install on stage. Pre-recorded 30-second fallback video cued and ready to play in under 10 seconds if anything breaks.
Test the install fresh on the facilitator's machine the morning of.

sourceRefs.label: Demo script: Let me show you

blocks.title: Your toolkit, same discipline
blocks.body: One optional fast lane on your own machine. One shared participant surface for the whole room. Watch me install the fast lane here so you see what it adds when your setup is ready.

items.title: One fast lane to install.
items.body: In your team's repo, if your local setup is ready. If not, the participant surface still carries the workshop.

items.title: Your brief, on demand.
items.body: If the skill is available, it knows your team, your project, and your phase. Ask it, and it speeds up what the participant surface already makes available.

items.title: Reference material when you're stuck.
items.body: Patterns, examples, checks. Not a tutorial — a reference you pull at the moment of need.

items.title: A place to record what you learn.
items.body: Every time you find a mistake worth fixing for next time, it lives here.

blocks.title: Hashimoto's rule, operationalized.
blocks.body: Anytime you find an agent makes a mistake, you take the time to engineer a solution such that the agent never makes that mistake again. This skill is where you do that — in a file, in the repo, for the next person. Or the next you.

scenes.label: Your first ten minutes
scenes.title: Your first ten minutes
scenes.body: In ten minutes I want every team at the point where the agent is actually helping you draft your plan. Here's how you get there — four moves. Don't skip any of them.
facilitatorNotes: Facilitator transition to Build 1: "Clock starts now. Build Phase 1 is open."

sourceRefs.label: Demo script: Let me show you

blocks.title: Your first ten minutes
blocks.body: Four moves. Don't skip any of them. In ten minutes the agent should be helping you draft the plan.

items.title: Open your team's repo.
items.body: Create from template if you need to. One person opens it for everyone.

items.title: Use the faster path if your setup is ready.
items.body: Install the workshop skill only if that is the fastest move for your team. If it breaks, keep moving from the participant surface and raise a hand.

items.title: Agree on what you're building.
items.body: Use the participant surface to open the prepared briefs, or propose your own. If your setup is ready, the skill is the faster path. Five minutes to decide, not fifty.

items.title: Start your AGENTS.md with the agent.
items.body: Open a session and draft three things together: what done looks like, what's not in scope, what you'll verify first. The agent is your first collaborator. Let it ask you questions.

blocks.title: Align with your team, then align with the agent.
blocks.body: Your team has to know what it wants before you can ask the agent for it. Five minutes of team alignment saves an hour of drift.

participantMoments.label: Watch the contrast
participantMoments.title: Watch what the repo changes
participantMoments.body: Stay with the demo. The point is not the command. The point is which repo artifact changes the agent's behavior.

blocks.title: Watch what the repo changes
blocks.body: Stay with the contrast. Look for the artifact that changes the outcome.

participantMoments.label: Name your first artifact
participantMoments.title: Decide what your team will create first
participantMoments.body: You are about to copy this flow. Choose the first repo artifact your team must create before implementation expands.

blocks.title: Decide what your team will create first
blocks.body: AGENTS.md, a plan, a tracer, or another explicit contract. Name it before Build 1 starts.

participantMoments.label: Open the working materials
participantMoments.title: Open the brief, repo, and fallback path now
participantMoments.body: Build 1 starts next. Open the brief, open the repo, and keep the browser fallback visible if local setup is not ready.

blocks.title: Open the brief, repo, and fallback path now
blocks.body: Use the participant surface as the stable path. Use the skill only if it is genuinely faster for your table.

phases.label: Build fáze 1
phases.goal: Get every table to a real before-lunch baseline: a navigable repo, AGENTS.md, a plan, one explicit check, and a first reviewed slice of work.
phases.roomSummary: The facilitator should keep the milestone board visible, coach through questions, and keep pushing teams away from unbounded generation and toward explicit verification.
facilitatorPrompts: Coach first, mentor second, teach only as a last resort.
If the team has no verification at all, push for the smallest useful test or tracer bullet.
Return learner-facing artifacts to participants, not the whole backstage of Harness Lab.
watchFors: The team keeps generating text, but verifies nothing.
AGENTS.md grows into a warehouse for everything.
It is unclear what is done, what is in progress, and what is only a hypothesis.
checkpointQuestions: Does the team have one shared understanding of the goal?
Is context accumulating in the repo, or staying in heads and chat?
Could another team find the first safe move in five minutes?

facilitatorRunner.goal: Get every table to a real before-lunch baseline: a navigable repo, AGENTS.md, a plan, one explicit check, and a first reviewed slice of work.
say: Coach first, mentor second, teach only as a last resort.
If the team has no verification at all, push for the smallest useful test or tracer bullet.
Return learner-facing artifacts to participants, not the whole backstage of Harness Lab.
An agent that starts without constraints will make plausible but wrong architectural decisions. This is task drift. AGENTS.md constraints are the fix.
watch: The team keeps generating text, but verifies nothing.
AGENTS.md grows into a warehouse for everything.
It is unclear what is done, what is in progress, and what is only a hypothesis.

scenes.label: Clock started
scenes.title: Clock started. You're on.
scenes.body: Team first. Map first. Tracer first. Facilitators are walking — ask for questions, not answers. If you get stuck, the recovery pattern is one flip away. At 11:35, we pause together.
facilitatorNotes: 30-second beat. Say "go" out loud while this is on screen, then flip to 4.2.

blocks.title: Clock started. You're on.
blocks.body: Team first. Map first. Tracer first. Facilitators are walking — ask for questions, not answers. At 11:35, we pause together.

scenes.label: The next 65 minutes
scenes.title: The next 65 minutes
scenes.body: Here's the arc your team is building toward. You don't have to hit every beat at the exact minute. But by lunch, your repo should hold the full shape of the work — not your conversations, not your heads, the repo.
facilitatorNotes: Default ambient scene for Build 1 — stays on the big screen and participant boards unless facilitator flips to 4.3.

blocks.title: The next 65 minutes
blocks.body: Here's the arc. Follow the shape. Do not chase every possible feature.

items.title: 10:30–10:40 · Set up.
items.body: Repo open. Skill installed. Team aligned on what you're building. First AGENTS.md draft started with the agent.

items.title: 10:40–11:00 · Plan and scaffold.
items.body: AGENTS.md as a map. A short plan. A first tracer sketched before the code.

items.title: 11:00–11:20 · Ship the first slice.
items.body: One bounded piece of work. One tracer that runs. Commit it.

items.title: 11:20–11:35 · Re-run, clean up, note the next move.
items.body: If the tracer fails, fix it. If it holds, make the repo readable for the next team.

scenes.label: By lunch
scenes.title: By lunch, the repo should carry the work
scenes.body: The point is not just code before lunch. It is a repo another team can continue.
facilitatorNotes: Use this scene when the room needs the baseline restated, or when the timeline scene has already done its job.

blocks.title: By lunch, the repo should carry the work
blocks.body: Map, plan, proof, next move. Not in your conversations. In the repo.

items: A short plan someone else can follow.
One working slice plus a tracer that proves it runs.
A next-move note left in the repo.

blocks.title: Holistic beats granular.
blocks.body: Start with the smallest end-to-end proof you can actually run.

scenes.label: Return to the proof
scenes.title: Return to the proof
scenes.body: More prompting won't unstick you. Step back. The answer is already in one of three places.
facilitatorNotes: Flip to this scene when a team is stuck, or project room-wide at a checkpoint moment.
Coach first, mentor second, teach only as a last resort.

blocks.title: Return to the proof
blocks.body: More prompting won't unstick you. Step back. The answer is already in one of three places.

items.title: State what you're trying to prove.
items.body: Out loud, at the table. What are you actually trying to do? How will you know you moved forward? If the team can't answer, that is the block.

items.title: Find the blind spot in the repo.
items.body: Is the problem in the README? AGENTS.md? The plan? The verification? The place the repo can't answer your question is the place to fix.

items.title: Add the smallest check.
items.body: A test. A tracer bullet. A one-line script that proves reality matches your mental model. Any executable signal beats more guessing.

blocks.title: If a facilitator hands you an answer, it didn't help.
blocks.body: Ask for a question, not an answer. If you leave the conversation with a spoken solution but no repo artifact, the problem will come back an hour later. Return the fix to the repo.

phases.label: Intermezzo 1
phases.goal: Make the first round of learning visible across the room and reconnect teams to the discipline behind the output, not only to the output itself.
phases.roomSummary: Each team gives one tight signal. The facilitator then connects repo evidence, room observations, and one principle point grounded in what is actually happening.
facilitatorPrompts: What did you change and why?
What did you move from chat or from your head into the repo?
What are you verifying today with an executable check?
watchFors: The intermezzo slips into reporting without a principle point.
Only the loudest team speaks while the others stay passive.
The facilitator summarizes impressions instead of evidence.
checkpointQuestions: What did you move from your head or chat into the repo?
What are you verifying today with an executable check?
What should the next team read first?

facilitatorRunner.goal: Make the first round of learning visible across the room and reconnect teams to the discipline behind the output, not only to the output itself.
say: What did you change and why?
What did you move from chat or from your head into the repo?
What are you verifying today with an executable check?
do: Start with the silent retrieval scene. 3 minutes individual writing, then 2 minutes team discussion, then room sharing.
watch: The intermezzo slips into reporting without a principle point.
Only the loudest team speaks while the others stay passive.
The facilitator summarizes impressions instead of evidence.
If someone starts talking during the silent phase, redirect. The retrieval benefit requires individual production before social sharing.

scenes.label: Write before you speak
scenes.title: Write before you speak
scenes.body: Three minutes. Individual. No talking. Write whatever comes up — short, honest, unedited. The retrieval only works if you produce before you share.
facilitatorNotes: If anyone talks during the 3 min silent phase, redirect immediately.

blocks.title: Write before you speak
blocks.body: Three minutes. Individual. No talking. Write whatever comes up — short, honest, unedited.

blocks.title: The one question to write against.
blocks.body: What surprised you about working with the agent this morning — and what does that make you want to try differently next? Three minutes. Don't talk yet.

scenes.label: Your team's check-in
scenes.title: Your team's check-in
scenes.body: Five minutes at your table. Compare what you wrote. Then one person writes the team's check-in into the team card on your participant board. Short, honest — two or three sentences is fine. It becomes part of your team's record.
facilitatorNotes: Team card append-only check-in log is load-bearing here. The entry persists across the day.

blocks.title: Your team's check-in
blocks.body: Compare what you wrote. One person types the team's check-in into the team card. Short, honest — two or three sentences.

items.title: Share what you wrote (3 min).
items.body: Go around the table. Read what you wrote out loud to each other. No debate, just listen.

items.title: Agree on the check-in (1 min).
items.body: What's actually true about your team right now? What's surprising? What do you want to try differently?

items.title: One person writes it (1 min).
items.body: On the participant board, in the team check-in field. It saves automatically and becomes part of your team's record.

blocks.title: If it's not recorded, it didn't happen.
blocks.body: Same rule as the repo. A check-in spoken at the table and lost is not a check-in. Type it. Two sentences is enough. It will be here for your future self and for whoever reads this project next.

scenes.label: The thread
scenes.title: The thread
scenes.body: Your check-ins are on screen. I'm going to read them out loud, one team at a time. Then I'll pull the thread that connects them — one principle point for the morning.
facilitatorNotes: The principle point is ad hoc, read from the room. Don't pre-can it.
Uses the team-trail chrome preset to render all team check-in trails.

blocks.title: The thread
blocks.body: Your check-ins are on screen. I read them out loud, one team at a time. Then I pull the thread that connects them — one principle point for the morning.

items.title: Read the check-ins on screen.
items.body: Facilitator scrolls through each team's recorded check-in and reads it aloud. No debate, just listening.

items.title: The thread.
items.body: Facilitator names the one thing that connects the check-ins — a pattern, a tension, a shared gap, a common win. Grounded in what is literally on screen, not in impressions.

items.title: One thing to carry.
items.body: What each person takes into the afternoon. Verbal, or written back into the team card — facilitator's call.

blocks.title: The principle comes from the screen, not from vibes.
blocks.body: Every team's check-in is on screen. The thread is whatever is actually there, not what the facilitator hopes is there.

phases.label: Lunch
phases.goal: Interrupt the momentum before the afternoon shift and force teams to leave behind a repo another group can actually read.
phases.roomSummary: Before lunch, teams need to clean up context, record the next safe move, and leave the repo in a state that does not rely on their memory. Lunch is not a pause in the handoff constraint.
facilitatorPrompts: Handoff is not the end of the day. It is a design constraint throughout the day.
Write down what the next team should read first.
If the same rule was said aloud twice, it belongs in the repo.
watchFors: Teams leave for lunch without a clear next step and without cleaned-up context.
A key decision is still only in people’s heads.
The repo does not explain the midday state.
checkpointQuestions: What should the next team read first?
Does the repo show what is done, what is a hypothesis, and what the next safe move is?
What still lives only in conversation and must go into the repo?

sourceRefs.label: Talk: What I want them to adopt

facilitatorRunner.goal: Interrupt the momentum before the afternoon shift and force teams to leave behind a repo another group can actually read.
say: Handoff is not the end of the day. It is a design constraint throughout the day.
Write down what the next team should read first.
If the same rule was said aloud twice, it belongs in the repo.
do: During lunch, quietly check each team repo. If a repo is genuinely unusable (no AGENTS.md at all, no code, broken setup), help the team write the minimum. Frame it as normal coaching, not a gate.
watch: Teams leave for lunch without a clear next step and without cleaned-up context.
A key decision is still only in people’s heads.
The repo does not explain the midday state.
The gate is silent — participants should not know there is a check. Only intervene on genuinely broken repos, not weak ones.

scenes.label: Back at return time
scenes.title: Back at [return-time]
scenes.body: Eat. Walk. Breathe. See you at [return-time] sharp.
facilitatorNotes: The [return-time] placeholder is a facilitator-settable variable. Default is the scheduled resume time from Phase 1's day schedule; facilitator can override it live on the day via the scene editor.

blocks.title: Back at [return-time]
blocks.body: Eat. Walk. Breathe. See you at [return-time] sharp.

blocks.title: In case you forgot the morning's line.
blocks.body: Humans steer. Agents execute. — Ryan Lopopolo, OpenAI Frontier & Symphony

phases.label: Team rotation
phases.goal: Force a quiet start after rotation and let repo quality reveal what is genuinely legible.
phases.roomSummary: For the first ten minutes, the incoming team only reads the repo and maps the situation. Any confusion is diagnostic data about the quality of the context, not a workshop bug to be patched with talk.
facilitatorPrompts: The first 10 minutes are quiet. No improvised explanation.
For the first 10 minutes, the new team only reads the repo and maps the situation.
Frustration is not a workshop bug. It is a signal of context quality in the repository.
watchFors: The original team starts explaining things verbally.
The new team edits the first file they open without a situation map.
The facilitator rescues a weak repo signal with spoken handoff.
checkpointQuestions: What helps, what is missing, what is risky, and what is the next safe move?
Does the repo make it clear where to look first?
What needs to be written, clarified, or verified after rotation?

facilitatorRunner.goal: Force a quiet start after rotation and let repo quality reveal what is genuinely legible.
say: The first 10 minutes are quiet. No improvised explanation.
For the first 10 minutes, the new team only reads the repo and maps the situation.
Frustration is not a workshop bug. It is a signal of context quality in the repository.
A new agent session has no memory of the previous session. If your context is in chat only, the session starts blind. This is why the repo is the session state.
watch: The original team starts explaining things verbally.
The new team edits the first file they open without a situation map.
The facilitator rescues a weak repo signal with spoken handoff.

scenes.label: Your repo is not yours anymore
scenes.title: Your repo is not yours anymore
scenes.body: For the rest of the day, you are working in the repo another team built this morning. Another team is working in yours. The team names — and the anchors that mark them — stay where they are. The people move. Everything you left in the repo at lunch is everything the new team has.
facilitatorNotes: The reveal beat. Keep it calm, direct.

blocks.title: Your repo is not yours anymore
blocks.body: For the rest of the day, you are working in the repo another team built this morning. Another team is working in yours. Everything you left in the repo at lunch is everything the new team has.

blocks.title: This isn't an ambush. It's the test.
blocks.body: The opening said you would hand off and inherit. Lunch was the gate. Whatever you left in the repo at noon is everything the new team has. Whatever is still in your heads — they will never hear it. This is what we practiced for.

scenes.label: Line up, count off, walk to the anchor
scenes.title: Line up, count off, walk to the anchor
scenes.body: I'm going to call a count-off — same shape as this morning. Line up at your old anchor, count off one through N, and remember your number. When I say go, everyone with the same number forms a new team at the anchor that matches. The repo, the history, and the check-in are already waiting.
facilitatorNotes: Physical movement. Enforce the silence rule gently but firmly.

blocks.title: Line up, count off, walk to the anchor
blocks.body: Same mechanic as this morning. Your number tells you which anchor to walk to.

items.title: Stand up at your old anchor.
items.body: Laptop, notebook, whatever you need — bring it with you.

items.title: Count off along your team.
items.body: One, two, three, four, five. Remember your number.

items.title: Walk to the anchor with your number.
items.body: All the 1s to Anchor 1, all the 2s to Anchor 2, and so on. The repo, the Intermezzo 1 check-in, and the team name are already there.

items.title: Introduce yourselves — properly (3–5 min).
items.body: Three things each: (1) your name and one thing you care about in software, (2) one thing you're bringing from your old team, (3) one thing you want to know about this new repo.

blocks.title: Your old repo is not yours anymore. Do not help.
blocks.body: You are leaving your old team's repo behind. The people arriving there have to read it cold. If they catch your eye, smile and point at the repo. The silence is the test — for you too.

scenes.label: Every fresh agent session is a rotation
scenes.title: Every fresh agent session is a rotation
scenes.body: A new agent has no memory of the previous session. A new teammate next month opens your repo the same way. Reading a stranger's repo cold is what every fresh agent session looks like. We're just practicing it out loud, with humans, at scale.
facilitatorNotes: This is where rotation stops being a gimmick and becomes daily practice.
Keep the emotional reframing short; the room mainly needs the quiet-start protocol and the identity shift.
If teams look uneasy, say the confusion is the signal out loud instead of putting another paragraph on the screen.

blocks.eyebrow: Now you sit in the other team's work
blocks.title: Every fresh agent session is a rotation
blocks.body: Reading a stranger's repo cold is what every fresh agent session looks like. That is the move you are about to practice.

items.title: Read (5 min).
items.body: README, AGENTS.md, the plan, the latest commits, the team's Intermezzo 1 check-in. No keyboard. No conversation with anyone from the original team.

items.title: Diagnose, record, then start (5 min).
items.body: At the new table, name what helps, what's missing, what's risky, and the next safe move. Write that diagnosis on the team card, include the new team composition, then start from the safest proof you can see.

phases.label: Build Phase 2 · first push
phases.goal: Continue from repo-native signals only, then turn the friction of the handoff into stronger maps, stronger checks, and stronger continuation guidance.
phases.roomSummary: The second build phase is not simply more implementation. It is the test of whether the repo and the agent workflow can carry a genuinely new team.
facilitatorPrompts: Do not let teams replace a weak repo signal with verbal handoff.
Name what needs to be written, clarified, or verified after rotation.
Every repeated pain is a candidate for a better template, check, or runbook.
watchFors: The team only cleans up chaos, but does not turn it into better guidance.
A weak repo signal keeps getting rescued in conversation.
There is no explicit new verification after the handoff.
checkpointQuestions: Which repo signal helped you continue the most?
What do you need to write or clarify after rotation?
Which next check do you need now so you can continue safely?

sourceRefs.label: Facilitation guide: Facilitation point for rotation

facilitatorRunner.goal: Continue from repo-native signals only, then turn the friction of the handoff into stronger maps, stronger checks, and stronger continuation guidance.
say: Do not let teams replace a weak repo signal with verbal handoff.
Name what needs to be written, clarified, or verified after rotation.
Every repeated pain is a candidate for a better template, check, or runbook.

When the agent generates tests as part of the same task, those tests reflect the agent interpretation, not your specification. Independent verification means: you wrote the done criteria, or a separate check evaluates the output.
do: Start with the codification pause. 5 minutes. Only after teams have encoded at least one friction point do they continue building.
watch: The team only cleans up chaos, but does not turn it into better guidance.
A weak repo signal keeps getting rescued in conversation.
There is no explicit new verification after the handoff.

scenes.label: Same clock, new context
scenes.title: Same clock, new context
scenes.body: You're at a new anchor, with a new team, in a new repo. Ten minutes of reading first — no keyboard. Then capture what tripped you, so the next hour is clean. Then build. Eighty-five minutes of work, split by a twenty-minute pause at 14:30. Facilitators walking, recovery one flip away.
facilitatorNotes: 30-second kickoff. Mirrors Scene 4.1.

blocks.title: Same clock, new context
blocks.body: Ten minutes of reading first — no keyboard. Then capture what tripped you, so the next hour is clean. Then build.

scenes.label: First push
scenes.title: First push: inherit, clean, ship
scenes.body: Build Phase 2 starts with reading and codifying friction. The second push has its own scene after the intermezzo.
facilitatorNotes: Default ambient scene for Build 2 first push.
The second half now lives in the next phase, not on this card.
Keep verification independent, but coach it verbally instead of spending a full on-screen callout on it.

blocks.title: First push: inherit, clean, ship
blocks.body: Same goal as this morning. One new move: capture friction fast so the first push starts clean.

items.title: 13:45–14:00 · Read and capture friction.
items.body: No keyboard at first. Read the repo, write the diagnosis and new team composition on the team card, then patch the biggest missing guide, rule, or check.

items.title: 14:00–14:30 · First push — ship a slice.
items.body: Thirty minutes. One bounded piece. One tracer that runs. Commit it.

items.title: 14:30–14:50 · Mid-point pause (Intermezzo 2).
items.body: Twenty minutes. Step back with the room, check in, then come back for the second push.

scenes.label: Return to the proof
scenes.title: Return to the proof
scenes.body: More prompting won't unstick you. Step back. The answer is already in one of three places.
facilitatorNotes: Same content as Scene 4.3. Creates rhythm so the room recognizes the recovery pattern across both builds.

blocks.title: Return to the proof
blocks.body: More prompting won't unstick you. Step back. The answer is already in one of three places.

items.title: State what you're trying to prove.
items.body: Out loud, at the table. If the team can't answer, that is the block.

items.title: Find the blind spot in the repo.
items.body: The place the repo can't answer your question is the place to fix.

items.title: Add the smallest check.
items.body: A test. A tracer bullet. Any executable signal beats more guessing.

blocks.title: If a facilitator hands you an answer, it didn't help.
blocks.body: Ask for a question, not an answer. Return the fix to the repo.

phases.label: Intermezzo 2
phases.goal: Capture what truly helped after rotation and what is emerging as a weak point in the room’s current way of working.
phases.roomSummary: The second intermezzo should pull concrete continuation signals into the open and prepare the room for reveal and reflection without drifting into vague retrospectives.
facilitatorPrompts: What helped you continue after rotation?
What was missing or slowing you down?
Which repo signal saved you the most time?
watchFors: The intermezzo stays general and without concrete examples.
You collect opinions, not signals from the repo.
No link appears to the closing reflection.
checkpointQuestions: What helped you continue after rotation?
What was missing?
Which repo signal saved you the most time?

facilitatorRunner.goal: Capture what truly helped after rotation and what is emerging as a weak point in the room’s current way of working.
say: What helped you continue after rotation?
What was missing or slowing you down?
Which repo signal saved you the most time?
do: Start with the silent retrieval scene. 3 minutes individual writing, then 2 minutes team discussion, then room sharing.
watch: The intermezzo stays general and without concrete examples.
You collect opinions, not signals from the repo.
No link appears to the closing reflection.
If someone starts talking during the silent phase, redirect. The retrieval benefit requires individual production before social sharing.

scenes.label: Write before you speak
scenes.title: Write before you speak
scenes.body: Three minutes. Individual. No talking. Same rule as the first intermezzo — you produce before you share. This is a pause, not an ending. Write something you'd actually want to act on in the next forty minutes.
facilitatorNotes: Same silent-retrieval discipline as Intermezzo 1.

blocks.title: Write before you speak
blocks.body: Three minutes. Individual. No talking. This is a pause, not an ending.

blocks.title: The one question to write against.
blocks.body: What surprised you about the inherited repo so far — and what do you want to try differently in the second push? Three minutes. Don't talk yet.

scenes.label: Your team's check-in
scenes.title: Your team's check-in
scenes.body: Five minutes at your table. Compare what you wrote. Then one person writes the team's check-in on your team card. Short, honest — two or three sentences is fine. Another entry in the story the card is telling.
facilitatorNotes: This is not the final check-in. Another entry in the append-only log.

blocks.title: Your team's check-in
blocks.body: Compare what you wrote. Append to the team card. Two or three sentences.

items.title: Share what you wrote (3 min).
items.body: Go around the table. Read it out loud. No debate.

items.title: Agree on the check-in (1 min).
items.body: What does your team want to try in the second push?

items.title: One person writes it (1 min).
items.body: On the participant board, appended to the team card alongside the Intermezzo 1 check-in and the Build 2 first-beat diagnosis.

blocks.title: This is one of several check-ins.
blocks.body: The team card is accumulating a story. Intermezzo 1 check-in, rotation diagnosis, now this, and maybe more. Don't treat it like a final report. Treat it like a commit message — a small honest note about where your team is right now.

scenes.label: Back to the work
scenes.title: Back to the work
scenes.body: Your check-ins are on screen. I'll read them, pull the thread, and send you back to your team with one thing to try in the second push.
facilitatorNotes: Don't let Intermezzo 2 become a mini-Reveal. Keep it brisk.

blocks.title: Back to the work
blocks.body: I read your check-ins, pull the thread, and send you back with one thing to try in the second push.

items.title: Read the check-ins on screen.
items.body: Facilitator scrolls through each team's check-in and reads it aloud. Same rhythm as the first intermezzo.

items.title: The thread.
items.body: Facilitator names the shared pattern across the first push — what teams are discovering about the inherited repo, where the friction is concentrated. Grounded in what is literally on screen.

items.title: Back to work.
items.body: No extended reflection, no closing framing. Closing line: "Forty more minutes. Back to your teams."

blocks.title: This is a pause, not an ending.
blocks.body: Same rhythm as the first intermezzo — retrieve, share, move on. No carrying home yet. That is for later. Right now: what is working, what is not, and what you want to do in the next forty minutes.

phases.label: Build Phase 2 · second push
phases.goal: Apply the intermezzo's signal. Iterate, extend, push further. Commit and make the repo readable for Reveal.
phases.roomSummary: Second push of Build 2. The team acts on what it surfaced in the intermezzo and readies the repo for Reveal.
facilitatorPrompts: You just paused. Now act on the signal.
Commit what holds. Make the repo readable. Decide what you'll show at Reveal.
watchFors: Teams revert to overbuilding instead of closing the slice.
Teams skip the final cleanup and arrive at Reveal with a repo nobody can read.
checkpointQuestions: What did the intermezzo tell you? Did you act on it?
Is the repo readable by a stranger right now?
What will you show at Reveal?

facilitatorRunner.goal: Hold the tempo from the first push. The work is the same; the test is whether the team can act on the intermezzo signal.
say: Forty minutes. Back to the work.
Apply what you surfaced in the intermezzo.
At 15:30, commit and decide what you'll show.
show: Ambient timeline scene. Recovery pattern one flip away.
do: Walk the room. Watch for teams skipping the final cleanup.
watch: Teams over-extend instead of closing the slice cleanly.
Self-validation trap re-appearing in the second push.
fallback: If a team loses the thread, flip to the recovery scene and coach them back to the proof.

scenes.label: Clock resumes
scenes.title: Second push. Forty minutes. Back to the work.
scenes.body: Apply whatever you surfaced in the intermezzo. Iterate, extend, push further. At 15:30, commit, make your repo readable, decide what you'll show at Reveal.
facilitatorNotes: 30-second beat. Keep the tempo the room already knows from Build 1 and Build 2 first push.

blocks.title: Second push. Forty minutes. Back to the work.
blocks.body: Apply whatever you surfaced in the intermezzo. Iterate, extend, push further. At 15:30, commit and decide what you'll show at Reveal.

scenes.label: The second push
scenes.title: Forty minutes left. Make it count.
scenes.body: The work is the same discipline. The test now is whether you can take the signal from the intermezzo, sharpen the same story, and land it cleanly for Reveal.
facilitatorNotes: Default ambient scene for the second push.
This should feel like a pair with the first push: same clock, narrower decision.
If teams drift into a brand new direction, pull them back to the smallest reveal-worthy proof.

blocks.title: Forty minutes left. Make it count.
blocks.body: Same discipline as the first push. This time, use the intermezzo to sharpen the same story and land it.

items.title: 14:50–15:20 · Build the second proof.
items.body: Thirty minutes of building. Apply what the intermezzo surfaced. Extend only what the signal justifies.

items.title: 15:20–15:30 · Land the reveal slice.
items.body: Ten minutes. Commit. Make the repo readable. Pick the one thing you will show at Reveal.

blocks.title: Don't open a new front.
blocks.body: Use the intermezzo to sharpen the same story, not to start a different one. If you get lost, return to the smallest proof.

phases.label: Reveal and reflection
phases.goal: Close the day by naming the signals that helped the work carry forward through handoff and by turning them into next practice, not just a pleasant ending.
phases.roomSummary: Reveal and reflection are not a team ranking. They are the moment where the room decides which signals carried the work through handoff and which ones repeatedly failed.
facilitatorPrompts: We are not judging which team was better.
We are looking at the system: which signals help work survive handoff and which ones slow it down.
Collect concrete examples, not general impressions.
watchFors: The reflection turns into ranking teams.
The questions stay too general and detached from the repo.
No next step emerges for next week or for improving the blueprint.
checkpointQuestions: What helped you continue?
What was missing?
What will you do differently next week?

facilitatorRunner.goal: Close the day by naming the signals that helped the work carry forward through handoff and by turning them into next practice, not just a pleasant ending.
say: We are not judging which team was better.
We are looking at the system: which signals help work survive handoff and which ones slow it down.
Collect concrete examples, not general impressions.
These practices are tool-agnostic. The next time you pick up any toolchain, the principles transfer: context in tracked artifacts, plan before motion, verification before trust.
do: After W3, run the next-day commitments: each person writes one sentence. Collect them.
watch: The reflection turns into ranking teams.
The questions stay too general and detached from the repo.
No next step emerges for next week or for improving the blueprint.

scenes.label: Alone, pairs, fours, all
scenes.title: Alone, pairs, fours, all
scenes.body: Four layers of reflection. Each one a little wider than the last. This is the most important twenty minutes of the day — do not rush it.
facilitatorNotes: Full 1-2-4-All. Real time on every layer (3 + 4 + 5 + 10 min).

blocks.title: Alone, pairs, fours, all
blocks.body: Four layers of reflection. Each one a little wider than the last.

items.title: Alone · 3 minutes.
items.body: Individual writing. What signal helped you continue after rotation? What was missing? What's the one thing from today you actually want in your own work next time?

items.title: Pairs · 4 minutes.
items.body: Turn to one neighbor. Compare what you wrote. Find the one thing between the two of you that's most worth sharing.

items.title: Fours · 5 minutes.
items.body: Two pairs become a four. Compare again. Find the signal, the gap, or the insight that the four of you think the whole room needs to hear.

items.title: All · 10 minutes.
items.body: One thing per group of four, shared with the whole room. Brief. Specific. Honest. No summaries, no rankings.

blocks.title: This isn't about winners.
blocks.body: We're looking for the signals that helped work survive handoff and the ones that still break under pressure. Every repeated pain across teams is a candidate for a better template, check, or runbook — not a team failure.

scenes.label: Show us what you built
scenes.title: Show us what you built
scenes.body: Each team has five minutes. You just spent twenty minutes articulating what you learned — now show us what carries it. Run the tracer. Walk the repo. Say the thing that surprised you most.
facilitatorNotes: Five minutes per team, no Q&A during demos. Hold questions for after.
With 6+ teams, decide between 5 min each (runs long) or 4 min each (tightest).

blocks.title: Show us what you built
blocks.body: Five minutes per team. Share your screen. Run what runs. Walk the repo.

items.title: One team at a time, five minutes each.
items.body: Share your screen. Run what runs. Walk the repo. Point at something specific.

items.title: Three beats for each demo.
items.body: The running code or repo walk (most of the five minutes). The single signal you wished had been in this repo when you arrived. The single thing you're proud of leaving behind.

items.title: No Q&A during demos.
items.body: Hold questions for after. Each team gets their five minutes clean.

blocks.title: The reflection did the work.
blocks.body: You spent twenty minutes in the 1-2-4-All figuring out what you learned. The demo isn't about finding words — you already have them. It's about showing the thing the words point at.

scenes.label: What I saw today
scenes.title: What I saw today
scenes.body: Now my part. I walked the room all day. I read every team's card as the afternoon moved. Here's what I saw from outside — the patterns, the surprises, the things everyone collectively figured out that maybe nobody felt individually.
facilitatorNotes: This is the hardest beat in the day. Prepare by reading team cards during the pre-Reveal buffer, not by preparing generic remarks.
Uses the team-trail chrome preset so facilitator can point at specific team card entries as evidence.

blocks.title: What I saw today
blocks.body: Two or three patterns, one or two surprises, and the craft observation — what this group did well. Grounded, specific, honest. Not a pep talk.

blocks.title: Evidence, not praise.
blocks.body: The facilitator's observations should be grounded in what's on the team cards and what was just shared in the 1-2-4-All and the demos. Not general encouragement. Specific signals, specific surprises, specific patterns. If you can't point at it, don't say it.

scenes.label: The one thing you'll change
scenes.title: The one thing you'll change
scenes.body: One sentence. Specific enough that you could tell, a week from now, whether you actually did it. Write it first. Say it out loud.
facilitatorNotes: Writing scene only. Storage moves to the next scene.
No Monday framing — anchored to "the next time I work with an agent."

blocks.title: The one thing you'll change
blocks.body: One sentence. Specific enough that you could tell, a week from now, whether you actually did it.

items.title: Write the sentence (2 min).
items.body: Format: "The next time I work with an agent, the first thing I'll change is [specific action] — because [specific reason from today]." No vague language. No "I will try to…"

items.title: Say it out loud at your table (3 min).
items.body: One person at a time. No debate, no defense. Just read it.

blocks.title: Specific enough to check.
blocks.body: Not: "I'll work better with agents." Yes: "The next time I start an agent task, I'll write Goal, Context, Constraints, and Done-When in AGENTS.md before typing anything into the prompt." If you can't tell a week from now whether you did it, the sentence isn't specific enough yet.

scenes.label: Save the commitment
scenes.title: Put it where you'll see it next time
scenes.body: The sentence only matters if you can find it when the next session starts.
facilitatorNotes: Storage scene. Participants pick one home and do it now.

blocks.title: Put it where you'll see it next time
blocks.body: Pick one place and store it now. Do not trust memory.

blocks.title: Four options — pick one.
blocks.body: Pick whichever actually fits how you work: (1) The workshop skill on your machine — save the commitment with the skill's notes command. (2) A physical card. (3) An email to yourself. (4) Wherever you already keep your own notes. Pick one. Do not pick none.

blocks.title: Tool-agnostic.
blocks.body: Everything you practiced today works with any coding agent. The repo matters. The verification matters. The handoff matters. The tool doesn't.

scenes.label: Go use it
scenes.title: Go use it
scenes.body: This is where my part ends and yours continues. You built something today that a stranger continued without you — that was the test, and you passed. The craft you practiced is yours. The materials stay with you.
facilitatorNotes: 30-60 seconds of screen time. The facilitator's closing words are more important than what's on the slide.
No "see you on Monday." No pep talk. The room feels more when the closing undersells.

blocks.title: Go use it
blocks.body: This is where my part ends and yours continues. You built something today that a stranger continued without you — that was the test, and you passed. The craft you practiced is yours. I won't be in the room next time you open an agent session — but everything you need is already in your hands.

blocks.title: The follow-up package.
blocks.body: The workshop skill and reference materials live with you after today. When you want to pull a pattern, a check, or a reminder, ask the skill. It knows what you learned because you were part of building it.

briefs.title: Code Review Helper
briefs.problem: Code review quality varies depending on who is looking. Some changes arrive with a sharp checklist and clear risk framing; others come through unevenly and the team loses consistency. Your job: design a tool that turns a diff into a usable review checklist — one that says what it is certain about, what is heuristic suggestion, and what still needs human judgment.
userStories: As a reviewer, I want a fast checklist of risks, questions, and focus areas from a diff.
As the author of a change, I want to know what I should verify before the review starts.
As the team after rotation, I want to continue from the heuristics the original team already discovered instead of reinventing them.
architectureNotes: This can be a CLI, web app, or simple script. The important part is a clear `diff -> analysis -> checklist` flow.
It must be obvious which inputs the tool expects and what it cannot evaluate reliably.
Add a seed diff or `examples/` so the workflow can be checked locally.
Separate heuristic from certainty clearly. The tool should help the reviewer, not pretend to be infallible.
acceptanceCriteria: Another team can add a new review rule within 10 minutes without reading through the whole codebase. *(Handoff test.)*
The tool produces a review checklist from a seed diff.
The output clearly separates certain findings from heuristic suggestions from items still needing human judgment.
The review rules themselves are readable and editable by someone who didn't write them.
There is at least one concrete example in `examples/` that demonstrates the full flow.
briefs.firstAgentPrompt: Don't start with code. First write the review rules, define what a good checklist looks like for a specific seed diff, and clarify what the tool *cannot* evaluate reliably. Only then propose the first implementation slice.

briefs.title: DevToolbox CLI
briefs.problem: Every team accumulates small one-off scripts — log cleaners, JSON parsers, commit lookups — that work for one person until nobody remembers how to run them. Your job: design a CLI that solves a few real developer pain points and survives handoff. Not a bag of scripts. A small system where the next command, test, and doc have an obvious home.
userStories: As a developer, I want to turn a log or JSON blob into a readable format with one command.
As a developer, I want to quickly find suspicious commits, branches, or changes without manually assembling git commands.
As a team, I want both the commands and the way of working documented so another team can continue after rotation without confusion.
architectureNotes: Choose any language or framework, but the CLI must stay easy to run and easy to discover.
Separate commands from helper utilities and configuration from the start.
`AGENTS.md` should describe the build and test flow, output conventions, and the rules for future extension.
A runbook for the team that inherits the project after lunch matters as much as a working command.
Do not build a bag of scripts. Build a small system where it is obvious where the next command, test, and doc belong.
acceptanceCriteria: Another team can add or fix a command within 10 minutes of opening the repo. *(Handoff test.)*
At least 3 working commands, each solving a concrete developer pain point.
`README` and `AGENTS.md` explain how to run and verify locally.
The extension pattern is visible — a new command fits without breaking the structure.
Every command has at least one readable input/output example.
briefs.firstAgentPrompt: Don't start with code. Start with `AGENTS.md`, a short plan for the extension pattern, and one clear verification step. Only then implement the first command.

briefs.title: Doc Generator
briefs.problem: Documentation goes stale almost immediately. Once maintenance is fully manual, the team postpones it, and after a few iterations nobody knows whether the docs still describe reality. After handoff, the next team no longer knows what to trust and what is only inference. Your job: design a tool that generates baseline technical documentation — or a structured project overview — from an existing codebase in a way that makes *what we know* and *what we're inferring* visibly different.
userStories: As a developer, I want basic technical documentation from a project without writing everything from scratch.
As a reviewer, I want to understand the module structure and main entry points within minutes.
As the team after rotation, I want to discover the architecture without long detective work.
architectureNotes: The input can be a local repo, seed directory, or simplified dataset.
The output can be Markdown, HTML, or a simple text report.
The important part is to explain what the tool can infer reliably and what remains heuristic.
Design the structure from the start so another output type can be added later.
Do not optimize for AI theatre. Optimize for trust, traceability, and a clear next safe move for a team after handoff.
acceptanceCriteria: A reviewer can read the generated documentation and tell exactly where each claim came from — within minutes, without opening the original source. *(Handoff test — provenance is the whole point.)*
Another team can add a new output format (Markdown → HTML, report → overview) without refactoring the whole tool.
The generated output makes it obvious which claims are *certain* (read directly from source) and which are *inference* (heuristic).
The tool produces at least one complete, readable output for a real or seed codebase.
`README` and `AGENTS.md` explain how to run locally and what input the tool expects.
briefs.firstAgentPrompt: Start by listing every signal the tool will read from a codebase (file names, imports, commit messages, directory structure, comments — whatever you're choosing) and for each one, decide whether it produces *certain* output or *heuristic* output. Write that spec in `AGENTS.md`. Only then propose the first implementation slice.

briefs.title: Metrics Dashboard
briefs.problem: Every team has the data — user signups, deploy frequency, error rates, feature usage — scattered across different tools. The shared screen that turns it into a decision-making view usually doesn't exist. Your job: design a dashboard that puts a few metrics and one trend on one screen, stays cleanly separated between data and UI, and is still legible after handoff to a team that didn't build it.
userStories: As a team, I want several metrics on one screen so the current state is legible quickly.
As a facilitator, I want to change seed data without touching UI logic.
As the team after rotation, I want to understand the data structure, components, and screens within minutes.
architectureNotes: Separate seed data from UI from the first commit.
Mobile-first is an advantage, but the dashboard must remain readable on a projected screen too.
README and monitoring should explain what already works, what is still mock, and what is still missing.
Design the structure so adding another metric does not require rewriting the whole screen.
Do not optimize only for the look. Make the data model, layout rules, and verification path easy for another team to understand.
acceptanceCriteria: A team that didn't build this can add a new metric within 10 minutes without breaking the layout. *(Handoff test.)*
The dashboard shows at least 3 metrics and one trend or comparison.
Seed data and UI logic are cleanly separated — a facilitator can swap seed data without touching the UI.
The layout stays legible on mobile and on a large projected screen; the `README` says how to test both.
The `README` documents which parts are real, which are mocked, and which are still missing.
briefs.firstAgentPrompt: Don't start with the UI. Start with the data model, the component boundaries, the layout rules, and a `Done When` in `AGENTS.md`. Only then touch any visual code.

briefs.title: Standup Bot
briefs.problem: Daily standups in chat become long threads where blockers get lost, dependencies between people are invisible, and nothing is reconstructable an hour later. Your job: design a tool that turns standup inputs into an overview a different team can continue working from — without the original author, without the original thread, and without a verbal handoff.
userStories: As a team lead, I want standup responses collected into one readable summary.
As a developer, I want to quickly see blockers, dependencies, and topics that need coordination.
As the team after rotation, I want to understand the data flow and integration points without verbal handoff.
architectureNotes: Prefer a clear data model over complicated integration.
Mock data is fine if the workflow feels realistic and is documented clearly.
Separate ingest, processing, and presentation of the output.
Prompts, runbooks, and decisions must live in the repo, not only in the heads of the original team.
Do not optimize for pretty summary prose before blockers, dependencies, and the next safe move are visible.
acceptanceCriteria: Another team can continue this project without a verbal explanation from the original team. *(Handoff test.)*
The tool ingests seed data in a documented format and produces an overview that surfaces blockers, dependencies, and items needing coordination.
The output distinguishes what the tool is certain about from what is only a heuristic suggestion.
The repo explains how to connect the ingest to a real input channel — without requiring it.
Ingest, processing, and presentation are cleanly separated in the code.
briefs.firstAgentPrompt: Don't start with code. Start with: the seed data format, the output data model, the rules that distinguish certainty from heuristic, and an `AGENTS.md` a rotating team will open first. Only then propose the first implementation slice.

challenges.title: Create AGENTS.md as a map
challenges.description: Write down the goal, build and test flow, durable rules, and where the next team should look first.

challenges.title: Write a code review skill
challenges.description: At least one repeatable review routine must live in the repo as a skill or runbook, not only in a prompt.

challenges.title: Use /plan before coding
challenges.description: Let the agent plan the work first, show what the plan was based on, and record the next safe move.

challenges.title: Add the smallest useful verification
challenges.description: Create a RED test, tracer bullet, or simple browser check before you give the agent more autonomy.

challenges.title: Run 2 parallel Codex sessions
challenges.description: Split the problem into two independent streams and compare what actually worked.

challenges.title: Add 'Done When' to every task
challenges.description: Every important task needs an explicit completion criterion and a link to verification.
